content:
Somewhere Along the Way, I Forgot Who Was Reading My Code

5 min read
Just now

--

When I started writing backend systems, I thought the compiler was my audience.
If the code compiled, passed tests, and ran fast enough, I considered the job done.
Variable names became one or two letters because they were “obvious.”
Functions quietly grew from twenty lines to two hundred.
Comments appeared everywhere because I knew nobody would understand what I had written six months later. Ironically, I was the first person who couldn’t understand it.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

AI generated imageI spent more time explaining the code than improving it.
Then one afternoon I had to debug a production issue in a Go service that I had written just eight months before.
It took me nearly forty minutes to understand what a single function was actually doing.
That was the moment I realised something.
The compiler had understood my code instantly.
The human who wrote it did not.
That human was me.
The Biggest Lie I Believed
“If It Works, It’s…
